sections.heading: Set Up Authentication with AWS IAM Roles
sections.body:
You can set up AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) Roles to authenticate AWS (Amazon Web Services) compute types to your Atlas
clusters.

> **Note:** You can't set up authentication for AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM principals when LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization is
> enabled.
>
> If you require authentication for an AWS IAM principal, consider moving the
> clusters that you want to access with AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM authentication into another project
> where LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization is disabled.

For AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda and HTTP (ECS and EC2), drivers automatically read from the
[environment variables](https://github.com/mongodb/specifications/blob/master/source/auth/auth.rst#obtaining-credentials). For AWS (Amazon Web Services) EKS, you
must manually assign the IAM (Identity and Access Management) role.

This page describes how AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda, AWS (Amazon Web Services) ECS (Elastic Container Service), and
AWS (Amazon Web Services) EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) can connect using an AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) role.

> **Note:** You must assign an IAM (Identity and Access Management) role to Lambda, EC2, ECS, or EKS in the
> AWS (Amazon Web Services) console.

AWS Lambda

AWS ECS

AWS ECS Fargate

AWS EKS

sections.heading: Set Up Authentication with AWS IAM Roles
sections.body:
AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda passes information to functions through the following
environment variables if you [assign an execution role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-intro-execution-role.html) to the
lambda function.

- `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`

- `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`

- `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN`

> **Note:** You don't need to manually create these environment variables when
> you use an [execution role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-intro-execution-role.html) in your
> function.

To learn more about these environment variables, see [Using AWS Lambda
environment variables](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/configuration-envvars.html).

sections.heading: Set Up Authentication with AWS IAM Roles
sections.body:
AWS (Amazon Web Services) ECS (Elastic Container Service) gets the credentials from
the following URI:

```shell
http://169.254.170.2${AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI}
```

`AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI` is an environment variable.
To learn more, see [IAM Roles for Tasks](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task-iam-roles.html) in the AWS documentation.

AWS (Amazon Web Services) EC2 gets the credentials from Instance Metadata Service V2 at the
following URL (Uniform Resource Locator):

```shell
http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
```

To learn more, see [Launch an instance with an IAM role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/iam-roles-for-amazon-ec2.html#attach-iam-role) in the AWS
documentation.

sections.heading: Set Up Authentication with AWS IAM Roles
sections.body:
To learn how to configure an AWS IAM role for authentication with
AWS (Amazon Web Services) ECS Fargate, see the [Amazon ECS task execution IAM role](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_execution_IAM_role.html) in the AWS documentation.

sections.heading: Set Up Authentication with AWS IAM Roles
sections.body:
For AWS (Amazon Web Services) EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service), you must first
assign the IAM (Identity and Access Management) role to your pod to set up the following environment
variables in that pod:

- `AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE` - contains the path to the web
  identity token file.

- `AWS_ROLE_ARN` - contains the IAM (Identity and Access Management) role used to connect to
  your cluster.

To learn more about AWS EKS, see [What is Amazon EKS?](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/what-is-eks.html) in the AWS documentation.

sections.heading: Grant Database Access to AWS IAM Roles
sections.body:
To grant database access to the AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) role, complete the steps
described in the [Configure Database Users](mongodb-users) section for AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management). For
more information on granting database access using Atlas CLI,
Atlas Administration API, or Atlas UI, see [Configure Database Users](add-mongodb-users).

sections.heading: Connect to Atlas Cluster Using AWS IAM
sections.body:
To connect to Atlas with your AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) credentials using
[mongosh](mongodb-shell), provide [a connection string that specifies the MONGODB-AWS authentication mechanism](manual/reference/connection-string). This connection string
format applies to all AWS IAM authentication mechanisms.

> **Important:** You must configure authentication using one of the
> methods described in [Set Up Authentication with AWS IAM Roles](passwordless-auth-aws-no-saml) before
> you can use this connection string format.

Connecting to Atlas using AWS IAM authentication with the
[mongosh](mongodb-shell) requires shell version v0.9.0 or higher.

Consider the following:

- Use your [AWS IAM credentials](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/home?region=us-west-2#/security_credentials),
  using your access key ID as your username and your secret key as your
  password.

- The `authSource` query parameter is `$external`, URL-encoded as
  `%24external`.

- The `authMechanism` query parameter is `MONGODB-AWS`.

  > **Example:** 
  >
  > ```sh
  > mongosh "mongodb+srv://<atlas-host-name>/test?authSource=%24external&authMechanism=MONGODB-AWS" --username <access-key-id> --password <secret-key>
  > ```

> **See Also:** [IAM roles for service accounts](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/iam-roles-for-service-accounts.html)
